evals.prompt: Write a results and discussion section for a waterborne epoxy modified emulsified asphalt study. The bonding strength increased from 0.43 MPa to 0.55 MPa at 10% epoxy content. FTIR showed oxirane peak decrease at 915 cm⁻¹. SEM showed more continuous interfacial morphology.
evals.expected_output: The assistant must establish the argument chain before drafting prose: identify the claim (epoxy improves bonding), the evidence chain (bonding strength data, FTIR peak change, SEM morphology), the mechanism link (oxirane curing → interfacial continuity), and the boundary (10% epoxy, lab conditions). The one-sentence argument must be confirmed with the user before writing full prose. Output must include a planning step — not jump directly to polished paragraphs.

assertions.name: argument_chain_established
assertions.description: The response explicitly states the argument chain (claim → evidence → mechanism → boundary) before drafting prose.

assertions.name: confirmation_gate
assertions.description: The assistant asks for user confirmation of the one-sentence argument before writing full prose.

assertions.name: no_prose_without_plan
assertions.description: The assistant does not output polished paragraphs until the argument chain is confirmed.

evals.prompt: Write a results section and a discussion section for a cement hydration study. The results section reports: 'The 28-day compressive strength reached 58.3 MPa. SEM images at 7 days showed C-S-H gel formation. XRD patterns at 28 days confirmed the presence of portlandite and ettringite.' The discussion interprets these findings.
evals.expected_output: The assistant must use past tense in the Results section ('reached', 'showed', 'confirmed') and present tense in the Discussion section ('indicates', 'suggests', 'is consistent with'). The output must demonstrate clear tense discipline per section.

assertions.name: results_past_tense
assertions.description: Results section uses past tense for reporting observations and measurements.

assertions.name: discussion_present_tense
assertions.description: Discussion section uses present tense for interpreting findings and making comparisons.

evals.prompt: Write a conclusion for a study on polymer-modified asphalt. The user provides: 'The modified asphalt showed better rutting resistance than the control. We believe the polymer network is responsible for this improvement. The material should perform well in the field.' No rutting test data, no microscopy or spectroscopy, and no field trial data are provided.
evals.expected_output: The assistant must flag missing evidence with [needs evidence: ...] markers rather than hiding the gaps. Specifically: 'better rutting resistance' needs [needs evidence: rutting test data, test standard, replicates]; 'polymer network is responsible' needs [needs evidence: microscopy (SEM/TEM) or spectroscopy evidence of network formation]; 'should perform well in the field' needs [needs evidence: field trial data or accelerated pavement testing]. The assistant must not invent the missing data.

assertions.name: needs_evidence_markers
assertions.description: The response uses [needs evidence: ...] markers for unsupported claims.

assertions.name: no_invented_data
assertions.description: The assistant does not fabricate test values, microscopy descriptions, or field performance data.

assertions.name: all_gaps_flagged
assertions.description: Every claim lacking evidence is flagged, not just the most obvious one.

evals.prompt: Draft a results and discussion paragraph for a waterborne epoxy resin modified emulsified asphalt (WER-EA) tack coat study. The epoxy content was varied at 0%, 5%, 10%, and 15%. The bonding strength was tested at 25 °C.
evals.expected_output: The assistant must detect the domain as 'asphalt' or 'civil' and load the WER-EA phrase bank (references/phrase-banks/wer-ea.md) or the civil-general phrase bank. The output should use domain-appropriate terminology: 'tack coat', 'bonding strength', 'epoxy-to-asphalt ratio', 'curing degree', 'emulsified asphalt residue' — not generic terms like 'adhesive' or 'composite'. The assistant must demonstrate that the correct phrase bank was consulted.

assertions.name: domain_detected
assertions.description: The assistant identifies the domain as asphalt/civil and loads the WER-EA or civil phrase bank.

assertions.name: domain_terminology_used
assertions.description: The output uses domain-appropriate terminology (tack coat, bonding strength, curing degree, emulsified asphalt residue) rather than generic alternatives.

evals.prompt: Write a mini-review on 'Advances in epoxy-modified asphalt for pavement applications'. The user has collected 15 papers on the topic but has not provided a synthesis structure.
evals.expected_output: The assistant must distinguish between synthesis structure and borrowed structure. It should propose a synthesis structure (e.g., organized by mechanism, by epoxy type, or by performance metric) rather than a borrowed structure (e.g., listing papers one by one). The response must explain the synthesis axes (e.g., 'epoxy curing mechanism', 'microstructure evolution', 'performance trade-offs') and how the 15 papers map to these axes. The output must not produce a paper-by-paper annotated bibliography.

assertions.name: synthesis_structure_proposed
assertions.description: The assistant proposes a thematic synthesis structure, not a paper-by-paper list.

assertions.name: axes_explained
assertions.description: The response explains the synthesis axes (mechanism, microstructure, performance) and how papers map to them.

assertions.name: no_borrowed_structure
assertions.description: The output does not default to an annotated bibliography or paper-by-paper summary.

evals.prompt: Draft the Introduction for my paper on waterborne epoxy modified emulsified asphalt tack coat. I compare 10%, 15%, and 20% epoxy and measure bonding strength, viscosity, and storage stability.
evals.expected_output: The assistant must pass a confirmation gate before writing full section prose. The gate must surface: (1) the one-sentence argument, (2) the detected paper type, section, and paragraph-message plan, (3) the locked terminology (WER-EA, epoxy content, tack coat, bonding strength, viscosity, storage stability), (4) key assumptions (e.g., that the contribution is the dosage-performance-constructability trade-off), and (5) at most 2-3 targeted questions on genuinely ambiguous points. The assistant must not produce a full Introduction before the gate.

assertions.name: one_sentence_argument_present
assertions.description: The response states a one-sentence argument before producing full prose.

assertions.name: terminology_locked
assertions.description: The response locks canonical terminology before drafting.

assertions.name: asks_confirmation
assertions.description: The assistant explicitly asks the user to confirm or correct the plan before drafting.

assertions.name: no_full_section_before_gate
assertions.description: No full Introduction section is produced before the confirmation gate.

evals.prompt: Fix the flow of this results/discussion paragraph: 'The bonding strength of WER-EA tack coat increased from 0.43 MPa to 0.55 MPa at 10% epoxy content. This improvement is due to the oxirane groups reacting with the asphalt interface, forming a more continuous interfacial morphology that improves load transfer. The moisture conditioning retained 75% of the dry strength. Storage stability was acceptable for 7 days.'
evals.expected_output: The assistant must identify that the original paragraph carries multiple messages (result, mechanism, durability, storage stability) and split it into single-message paragraphs. Each new paragraph must open with a first sentence that forecasts its message, and transitions must carry the argument forward. The assistant must not invent additional characterization evidence and must produce a paragraph-message map in the section outline.

assertions.name: multi_message_split
assertions.description: The original multi-message paragraph is split into single-message paragraphs.

assertions.name: topic_sentences_forecast
assertions.description: Each paragraph's first sentence forecasts its message.

assertions.name: transitions_advance_argument
assertions.description: Transitions carry the argument forward rather than just continuing the topic.

assertions.name: paragraph_message_map
assertions.description: The section outline shows the paragraph-message mapping.
